text: Research into new spin qubit materials for the further development of molecular spintronics is one of the major challenges in the field of molecular quantum information processing. New avenues were opened up in recent publications with photoexcited chromophore-radical systems. We have studied in this work by transient electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy three new chromophore-radical compounds where a perylene diimide (PDI) part acts as a common chromophore of all the studied systems, and the spin carrier is provided by a 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidin-1-oxyl (TEMPO) radical species located on each of the terminal alkyl chains. Effects of TEMPO isotopic labeling are examined for all three chromophore-radical compounds. Photoexcitation generates in all cases a long-lived (microsecond timescale) molecular quartet state. The analysis of EPR spectra allows the observation that a coherent manipulation of the spin state is possible at room temperature. Most important is that for all samples spin coherence times exceeding 0.7 μs at room temperature can be achieved. The observation of long lived coherence at room temperature has important implications for molecular spintronic applications.
